data.title: Why ServisFirst Bancshares Remains One Of My Preferred Regional Banks
data.description:
Thesis

I last wrote about ServisFirst Bancshares, Inc. (SFBS) a couple of years ago. It’s a Birmingham-based commercial bank with a clean balance sheet and a preference for old-fashioned, relationship-driven lending. At the time, I rated it a buy, explaining,

"...with a seemingly undervalued price-to-earnings ratio, it suggests an opportunity for investors to capitalize on a potentially undervalued stock that holds strong fundamentals."

And to be fair, that call worked.

Since my review was published, SFBS has risen about 64% and roughly 72% when including total returns. This clearly beats the S&P 500 (SP500), which gained about 50% over the same period.

In this update, after breaking down ServisFirst’s latest earnings, I’m maintaining my bullish view because execution remains strong and growth is holding up despite a shifting rate environment.

ServisFirst Bancshares Q4 Financial Performance

  • Revenue of $162.21M (22.91% Y/Y) beats by $10.41M
  • Full year earnings per share was $5.25 on an operating basis and $5.06 on a GAAP basis.
  • Net income available to common shareholders was $86.4 million for the quarter and $276.5 million for the year.

  • Loan growth was really in line with our pipeline projection with annualized growth of 12% for the quarter.
  • Our pipeline quarter-over-quarter increased by 11%, but net of projected payoffs had increased by 80%.
  • highlighted by a very busy fourth quarter of loan activity that produced an annualized growth rate of 12%.
  • I'd like to draw a particular attention to the nearly 10% growth in our C&I book during the year.

Let's start with loan growth, which has begun to pick up. Most of that performance came from having a stronger pipeline of opportunities and less pressure from loans being paid off early. Management says borrowers are holding off on refinancing, since Treasury yields keep moving around. And that lines up with what we’re seeing across the sector. More of the demand is now coming from businesses taking out loans for their day-to-day operations and growth, which is usually what happens when the economy isn’t rolling over just yet.

You could argue that most of that acceleration looks like a confidence trade tied to rate cuts. As financial conditions become less tight, borrowers who expect borrowing costs to fall are starting to take action. As a result, banks across the U.S. are seeing higher loan volumes. According to Reuters, overall sector growth reached 5.3% YoY by the end of the year.

  • We did continue to manage down our high-cost deposits, primarily of municipal deposits for both the quarter and the year. This allowed us to reduce our cost of interest-bearing liabilities by 40 basis points versus linked quarters and by 65 basis points versus the same quarter last year.
  • During this 2025 declining rate cycle, we experienced a strong deposit beta of 83 basis points.

On the deposit side, management cut back on expensive municipal deposits believing that, if loan demand returned, it could bring that money back easily. That pruning brought funding costs down, helped along by customers who took rate cuts with little resistance. It’s the same play regional peers like U.S. Bancorp (USB) ran to defend margins when the Federal Reserve started cutting rates in late 2025. The backdrop suggests banks are handling a steepening yield curve just fine for now. The open question is whether that discipline survives once deposit competition starts to heat up in a lower-rate world.

  • Our net interest margin experienced healthy growth throughout 2025, rising from 2.92% in the first quarter to 3.38% in the fourth quarter.
  • This expansion was driven by disciplined loan pricing, including a 40% increase in loan fee collection and boosted by deposit rate reductions in the fourth quarter.
  • we have right around $1 billion throughout 2026 that's going to reprice and the weighted average yield on those is 5.18%...compared to our going on rate of about 6.47%, then we got an opportunity to pick up 130 basis points or so on the loan side.

Net interest margin improved during the year, not just because the bank priced its loans carefully, but also because fee income increased. Management said this was directly tied to changes they made to banker incentive programs. At the same time, repricing low-yielding fixed-rate assets helped offset the automatic hit to variable-rate income as benchmarks moved lower.

  • Our asset yields remain strong at 5.79% for the quarter, which is down 3 basis points from the third quarter of 2025 and up 10 basis points from the first quarter of 2025.
  • Loan yields dropped slightly during the quarter to 6.30%, which was pleasing given the 75 basis point reduction in benchmark interest rates during the quarter.
  • We have floors on about 86% of our variable rate loans and the weighted average rate on those floors is 4.74%.

With the federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75%, the setup generally supports asset yields. The catch is that this only holds if execution is crisp, because the margin for error shrinks as soon as covenants start getting loosened or cash flows slow after even a mild economic hit. At the sector level, S&P Global suggests that net interest income could edge higher in 2026, conditional on loan growth in the 3–5% range. To be fair, the picture isn’t all clear skies. Policy risk has become a concern again, as possible regulatory changes, such as the administration’s proposal to cap credit card interest rates, could further squeeze the already thin profits in consumer lending.

Management also talked up early, hands-on problem solving, and in theory that sounds okay. It gets less persuasive if multifamily restructurings start to drag out, especially in CRE (commercial real estate markets) that are already dealing with too much supply, something I recently discussed when I covered WaFd Inc. (WAFD)

  • We have an additional $700 million roughly in cash flows and then we also talk about covenant valuations and loan modifications.
  • We see about at least in 2025, we saw about $300 million in repricing as a result of covenant violations and loan modification.
  • So all in, it's about a $2 billion opportunity we have going forward in the next 12 months.

Finally, liquidity remains ample, with no dependence on brokered deposits or wholesale advances, a stance that leaves the bank well positioned to pursue organic initiatives such as the build-out of its Houston team. The expansion is aimed squarely at C&I lending in Texas, built on existing correspondent relationships. The strategy makes sense to me on paper, but competition for talent could still push costs up, especially if more merger activity starts to disrupt the regional labor market.

SFBS's Valuation

The market clearly liked the results. At the time of my research, the stock's up about 13%, and the valuation has moved quickly with it. Right now, the stock trades at about 17x trailing earnings based on the updated numbers. But if consensus is right, and this year's EPS keeps growing in the high teens to maybe 20%, then I’d expect that multiple to come down. In that case, you’re probably looking at something like 12–13x forward earnings.

Meanwhile, the stock is already well above yesterday’s fair value call of $83. Price-to-book is now north of 2.3x, which is still expensive versus the sector around 1.3x, and price-to-sales hasn’t really come in either.

That said, it’s not hard to see why the stock carries a premium. Growth is doing most of the work. Revenue was up more than 20% this quarter, EPS growth held up, leverage stayed low, and credit trends still look clean.

But the macro risk is in play. If interest rates are cut or stay on hold, the bank’s NIM could be pushed lower, especially if deposit costs remain slow to decline. At the same time, competition in the Southeast and Texas is heating up on both loans and funding.

The dividend is still only about 2%, so the return case lives or dies on growth continuing to show up.

Final Takeaway

I'm keeping my Buy rating, even after today’s stock price increase. This was a strong quarter, loan growth's picking up speed (especially in C&I, loans), and there's a meaningful benefit coming from loan repricing this year, estimated at about a $2 billion opportunity. In addition, Texas expansion puts SFBS in a good position to continue generating high-teens EPS growth and to keep profit margins stable, even if interest rates stay lower.

data.title: Old National Bancorp: Just Ok, But Entering 2026 With Momentum
data.description:
We continue our regional bank Q4 earnings coverage with Old National Bancorp (ONB). This bank is operating fine, but there are some softening metrics. It is an average operator in our opinion, though it has been an acquirer of other banks over the years to fuel growth. We do know there has been past disappointing performance, though things are on the mend. From a shareholder return perspective in the medium-term, and as you can see from the chart, it was essentially flat in 2025.

Sure, if you timed a buy well, a rising tide lifted most boats. We got very bullish in April 2025 at BAD BEAT Investing on a range of sectors, including the banks. But for a stock to be flat after a great year for banks, while offering a paltry 2.5% yield, it is just not a strong performer.

Now, that does not mean the information contained in the earnings report is not valuable. And it does not mean the future is not looking bright. We like to analyze regional bank results to get a "pulse check" on local economies. It really helps us understand if we are seeing softening in the economy (or strength). Sure, while all banks have their own nuances, taken as a composite, we get a strong view of the U.S. economy in piecemeal fashion. So far, this reporting season, we have seen most banks grow net interest margin, earnings, loans, and deposits while asset quality has been mixed. That is on average. Let us examine the key metrics of Old National in the just-reported quarter.

Old National Bancorp Q4 headline results, loans, and deposits

On the top-line revenue figure, Old National Bancorp reported revenue of $690.6 million, rising 41% from last year, and beating estimates by $15 million. That was strong, and of course, the huge increase stems from aforementioned acquisition and merger activity. On the bottom line, EPS was a strong $0.62, surpassing estimates by $0.03.

Let us talk loans and deposits. We want to see growth here each quarter. Loans dipped on an annualized basis. Loans at the end of the quarter were up 6.4% annualized, but have been choppy the last few years. Growth over time is welcome organically, but quarter-to-quarter things continue to be mixed, with total period-end loans hitting $48.8 billion. Deposits were about flat annualized at $55.1 billion, with core deposits falling 3.2% annualized.

While there had been past issues with the commercial side of the business, this quarter actually saw respectable commercial loan production one again. The total commercial loan production in Q4 was a strong $3.5 billion and there was $4.8 billion in the pipeline. We see this as positive for the bank.

Net interest margin

Here in Q4, net interest income ticked up slightly to $588.8 million compared to $582.6 million in the sequential quarter. This was driven by lower funding costs, but the yields on interest earning assets were also lower. Margins were 3.65%, 1 basis point higher than the sequential Q3. Overall, this is a strong margin, all things considered. It is worth noting that the cost of funds dropped 17 basis points to 1.80%. This is a result of the Fed cutting rates. However, new loans are still being issued at much higher rates than most loans on the books.

Asset quality

We also like to examine the bank's asset quality trends. There was a loan loss provision expense of $32.7 million in the quarter, though relative to total loans on the books, this is not overly sizable. Of course, provisions do offset earnings power. Net charge-offs were $32.1 million, which was 0.27% of all loans. We would like to see this ratio lower. Further, 30+ day delinquencies were 0.22% of all loans, while nonaccrual loans are an elevated 1.07% of total loans. This is well above the regional bank average. Now, that is high, but we did see sequential improvement.

With better margins and an earnings beat, it was not surprising that the efficiency ratio improved to 51.6% from 58.8% in the sequential quarter. Further, the return on average assets actually improved 18 basis points from Q3 to 1.21%, while the return on average tangible equity was up 190 basis points to 17.8%. It was a decent quarter overall.

Final thoughts

Historically, this bank has had mixed performance, but remains a serial acquirer over the years to help fuel growth. Q4 was strong, and the bank enters 2026 flat from a year ago on the stock chart, but from a position of relative strength compared to its historical performance operationally. Loans were up, deposits were mixed, return metrics surged sequentially, and asset quality improved. Still, some of the asset quality metrics compared to your average regional bank give some pause, but overall the bank is fine. You can do better elsewhere in the sector, but remains a Hold based on this performance.
